preamble: The voiceless bilabial plosive /p/ can undergo a phonetic transformation to a glottal stop /ʔ/, a process often associated with weakening or debilaization, particularly in coda positions. While less frequent than the glottalization of /t/, this shift is attested in various languages and dialects.

examples.note: Proto-Chamic word-final *-p developed into a glottal stop, sometimes with an accompanying labial semivowel.
Proto-Chamic word-final *-p developed into a glottal stop, sometimes with an accompanying labial semivowel.
Coda /p/ can undergo glottalization, sometimes resulting in a full glottal stop, though this is less frequent than for /t/.

sources: O'Brien, J. (2010). Perception and English t-glottalization.
Di Napoli, J. (2018). Online perception of glottalized coda stops in American English. Laboratory Phonology, 9(1), 1–29.
Thurgood, G. (1992). Papers in Southeast Asian Linguistics No. 15: Chamic studies. Pacific Linguistics.
